title: Practice Set 9 — Production Pipeline Patterns
description: Build production-grade pipeline patterns: diagnose non-idempotent writes, refactor to idempotent overwrite-partition, implement incremental processing that handles late-arriving data and corrections, design backfill strategies, recover from mid-write failures, and build a complete incremental Silver pipeline with validation guards.

datasets.description: Existing Silver readings. 400 rows: 40 meters × 10 days (2026-03-01 to 2026-03-10).
Today's incremental batch. 50 rows: 35 new readings (2026-03-11), 10 late arrivals (2026-03-09), 5 corrections (2026-03-10, updated daily_kw).
Simulated double-run output: 500 rows = 400 existing + 50 batch + 50 duplicate batch. Demonstrates append-mode idempotency failure.

description.context: NordGrid's Silver pipeline uses append mode to write daily incremental data. On March 11th, the pipeline ran at 06:00 and appended 50 new rows. Due to a scheduler glitch, it ran again at 06:30 with the same input. The output now has 500 rows instead of 450 (400 existing + 50 new). The 50 duplicates inflate every downstream aggregation by ~11%.
description.task: Examine the pipeline code and the duplicated output below. Diagnose why the pipeline is not idempotent, calculate the impact on a SUM(daily_kw) aggregation, and identify the fix pattern.
description.validationHint: A pipeline is idempotent if running it twice with the same input produces the same result as running it once. Append mode is inherently non-idempotent.

codeBlock.label: Current pipeline (non-idempotent)
codeBlock.content:
# Daily incremental pipeline
df_batch = spark.read.csv('daily_batch/', header=True, inferSchema=True)

# BUG: append mode — re-running appends the same data again
df_batch.write.mode('append').parquet('silver/readings/')

# After first run:  400 existing + 50 new = 450 rows (correct)
# After second run: 450 + 50 duplicated = 500 rows (WRONG)

dupeEvidence.label: Duplicate detection query output
dupeEvidence.content:
SELECT meter_id, date, COUNT(*) as cnt
FROM silver_readings
WHERE date = '2026-03-11'
GROUP BY meter_id, date
HAVING cnt > 1

Result: 35 rows with cnt=2 (every March 11 reading appears twice)

SELECT date, SUM(daily_kw) as total
FROM silver_readings
GROUP BY date ORDER BY date DESC LIMIT 3

date       | total
2026-03-11 | 142,580.4  ← inflated (should be ~71,290)
2026-03-10 | 80,320.1   ← correct
2026-03-09 | 78,450.8   ← correct

fields.label: Why is this pipeline not idempotent?
options: Because Spark's append mode has a bug that creates duplicates
Because append mode blindly adds files to the output directory regardless of whether the data already exists. A re-run appends the same rows again, creating exact duplicates.
Because the daily batch contains duplicate meter_ids
Because the pipeline doesn't use explicit schemas
fields.correctAnswer: Because append mode blindly adds files to the output directory regardless of whether the data already exists. A re-run appends the same rows again, creating exact duplicates.

fields.label: What is the correct pattern for idempotent incremental writes?
options: Use append mode but deduplicate after each write
Use overwrite mode for the entire table (safe but rewrites everything)
Use overwrite mode with partitioning: overwrite only the affected date partition(s), leaving other partitions untouched. Re-running replaces the partition with the same data.
Use ignore mode to skip writes when data already exists
fields.correctAnswer: Use overwrite mode with partitioning: overwrite only the affected date partition(s), leaving other partitions untouched. Re-running replaces the partition with the same data.

fields.label: Why is overwrite-partition better than full-table overwrite for daily incremental pipelines?
options: There is no difference — both produce the same result

Full-table overwrite rewrites ALL 400 existing rows plus the 50 new rows every day. Overwrite-partition only rewrites the new date's partition (50 rows), leaving the 400 historical rows untouched. This is faster and safer.
Overwrite-partition is the only mode that supports Parquet
Full-table overwrite cannot be used with partitioned tables
fields.correctAnswer: Full-table overwrite rewrites ALL 400 existing rows plus the 50 new rows every day. Overwrite-partition only rewrites the new date's partition (50 rows), leaving the 400 historical rows untouched. This is faster and safer.

description.context: NordGrid's Silver pipeline needs to be refactored from append mode to the overwrite-partition pattern. The table is partitioned by date. When the daily batch arrives, the pipeline should overwrite only the date partitions that appear in the batch, leaving all other partitions untouched. This makes the pipeline idempotent: re-running produces the same result.
description.task: Refactor the pipeline to use partitionBy('date') with mode('overwrite') and the dynamic partition overwrite config. Write the batch data, then read back and union with existing data to verify. Store the complete table in a variable called silver.
description.validationHint: Set spark.sql.sources.partitionOverwriteMode to 'dynamic' so that overwrite only affects the partitions present in the DataFrame being written. Static mode (default) overwrites ALL partitions.

evidence.label: Current non-idempotent pipeline
evidence.content:
df_batch = spark.read.csv('PM9_data_readings_daily_batch.csv', header=True, inferSchema=True)
# BUG: append mode, no partition strategy
df_batch.write.mode('append').parquet('silver/readings/')

fields.label: What does spark.sql.sources.partitionOverwriteMode = 'dynamic' do?
options: Dynamically creates new partitions as data grows
When using mode('overwrite') with partitionBy(), only overwrites the partitions that appear in the DataFrame being written. Partitions not in the DataFrame are left untouched.
Automatically detects the partition column from the data
Enables dynamic resource allocation for partition writes
fields.correctAnswer: When using mode('overwrite') with partitionBy(), only overwrites the partitions that appear in the DataFrame being written. Partitions not in the DataFrame are left untouched.

starterScaffold.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col

spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .appName('nordgrid-idempotent-write') \
    .master('local[*]') \
    .getOrCreate()

# Load existing Silver data and write as partitioned Parquet
df_existing = spark.read.csv('PM9_data_readings_existing.csv', header=True, inferSchema=True)
df_existing.write.mode('overwrite').partitionBy('date').parquet('output/silver/')

# Load today's batch
df_batch = spark.read.csv('PM9_data_readings_daily_batch.csv', header=True, inferSchema=True)

print(f"Existing: {df_existing.count()} rows")
print(f"Batch: {df_batch.count()} rows")
print(f"Batch dates: {sorted([r.date for r in df_batch.select('date').distinct().collect()])}")

# YOUR SOLUTION BELOW
# 1. Set partitionOverwriteMode to 'dynamic'
# 2. Write the batch with mode('overwrite') and partitionBy('date')
# 3. Read back the complete Silver table
# 4. Run a second time to prove idempotency (same result)
# Store the complete table in 'silver'



# YOUR SOLUTION ABOVE

# Verification (do not modify)
print(f"Silver total rows: {silver.count()}")
print(f"Silver distinct dates: {silver.select('date').distinct().count()}")

assertions.description: Silver has correct row count (existing + new, no dupes)

tasks.title: Implement Incremental Processing with Late-Arrival Handling

description.context: NordGrid's daily batch contains three types of rows: new readings (2026-03-11), late arrivals (2026-03-09 readings that arrived late), and corrections (updated daily_kw for 2026-03-10). A naive incremental pipeline that only processes rows where date > max(existing_date) would miss the late arrivals and corrections. The pipeline must handle all three types correctly.
description.task: Design the incremental processing logic: (1) identify which dates in the batch overlap with existing data (for corrections/late arrivals), (2) process all batch rows regardless of date, (3) use the overwrite-partition pattern to replace affected date partitions. Store the final Silver table in a variable called silver.
description.validationHint: The batch has 3 distinct dates: 2026-03-09 (10 late), 2026-03-10 (5 corrections), 2026-03-11 (35 new). All three date partitions should be overwritten. The corrections replace the existing 2026-03-10 values for those 5 meters.

batchBreakdown.label: Daily batch composition (50 rows)
batchBreakdown.content:
Date       | Count | Type        | Action Needed
2026-03-11 | 35    | New         | Insert (new date partition)
2026-03-09 | 10    | Late arrival| Merge into existing 03-09 partition
2026-03-10 | 5     | Correction  | Replace existing values in 03-10 partition

naivePipeline.label: Naive pipeline (misses late arrivals)
naivePipeline.content:
# BUG: only processes rows after the max existing date
max_date = df_existing.agg({'date': 'max'}).collect()[0][0]  # 2026-03-10
df_new = df_batch.filter(col('date') > max_date)  # only 35 rows for 03-11
# LOST: 10 late arrivals for 03-09 and 5 corrections for 03-10

fields.label: Why does filtering batch rows to date > max(existing_date) fail?
options: Because Spark cannot compare date strings
Because late arrivals and corrections have dates that are already in the existing table. Filtering to only 'new' dates silently drops these rows, causing data loss.
Because max() returns null when the table is empty
Because the batch might have future dates
fields.correctAnswer: Because late arrivals and corrections have dates that are already in the existing table. Filtering to only 'new' dates silently drops these rows, causing data loss.

fields.label: What is the correct approach for handling all three batch types?
options: Process only new dates and discard late arrivals as too old

For each affected date partition: union the batch rows for that date with the existing rows for that date (excluding corrected keys), then overwrite the partition. This handles new, late, and corrected rows correctly.
Append everything and deduplicate afterward
Use MERGE for all three types in a single operation
fields.correctAnswer: For each affected date partition: union the batch rows for that date with the existing rows for that date (excluding corrected keys), then overwrite the partition. This handles new, late, and corrected rows correctly.
fields.distractorRationale: Option 4 (MERGE) is valid but requires Delta Lake. For Parquet-based pipelines, the union-and-overwrite-partition pattern is the standard approach. Option 3 creates duplicates until the dedup runs.

starterScaffold.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col

spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .appName('nordgrid-incremental') \
    .master('local[*]') \
    .config('spark.sql.sources.partitionOverwriteMode', 'dynamic') \
    .getOrCreate()

# Load existing Silver (partitioned by date)
df_existing = spark.read.csv('PM9_data_readings_existing.csv', header=True, inferSchema=True)
df_existing.write.mode('overwrite').partitionBy('date').parquet('output/silver/')

# Load today's batch
df_batch = spark.read.csv('PM9_data_readings_daily_batch.csv', header=True, inferSchema=True)

print(f"Existing dates: {sorted([r.date for r in df_existing.select('date').distinct().collect()])}")
print(f"Batch dates: {sorted([r.date for r in df_batch.select('date').distinct().collect()])}")

# YOUR SOLUTION BELOW
# Handle all three batch types correctly:
# 1. Identify affected dates in the batch
# 2. For affected dates: read existing partition, remove rows that 
#    match batch keys (meter_id, date), union with batch rows
# 3. Write affected partitions with overwrite-partition mode
# 4. Read back the complete Silver table
# Store in 'silver'



# YOUR SOLUTION ABOVE

# Verification (do not modify)
print(f"Silver total: {silver.count()}")
for d in sorted([r.date for r in silver.select('date').distinct().collect()]):
    cnt = silver.filter(col('date') == d).count()
    print(f"  {d}: {cnt} rows")

assertions.description: Date 2026-03-09 has late arrivals merged in

tasks.title: Design a Backfill Strategy for NordGrid's Silver Table

description.context: NordGrid discovered a bug in the Silver pipeline's region mapping: for 3 months (January–March 2026), NORTH_COAST readings were incorrectly mapped to NORTH. The bug is now fixed, but 3 months of Silver data (90 date partitions, ~100M rows) needs to be reprocessed from Bronze. The backfill must not disrupt the daily production pipeline that writes to the same Silver table.
description.task: Design the backfill strategy: how to partition the work, how to avoid conflicts with the daily pipeline, how to validate the backfill, and how to handle failures mid-backfill.
description.validationHint: The key constraint: the daily pipeline and the backfill cannot overwrite the same partition simultaneously. Date-level partitioning provides natural isolation: the daily pipeline writes today's partition, the backfill writes historical partitions.

backfillScope.label: Backfill requirements
backfillScope.content:
Affected dates: 2026-01-01 to 2026-03-31 (90 days)
Rows per day: ~1.2M
Total rows to reprocess: ~108M
Source: Bronze Parquet (partitioned by date)
Target: Silver Parquet (partitioned by date)
Bug: region mapping NORTH_COAST → NORTH (fixed in pipeline code)

Constraints:
- Daily pipeline runs at 06:00, writes today's date partition
- Backfill must not interfere with daily writes
- Cluster has 20% spare capacity during 08:00–18:00
- If backfill fails mid-way, partial progress should be preserved

fields.label: How should the backfill work be partitioned?
options: Reprocess all 90 days in a single Spark job
Process one date partition per job, running 90 sequential jobs. Each job reads one day from Bronze, transforms with the fixed code, and overwrites that day's Silver partition.
Process all 90 days in parallel in a single job with repartition(90)
Process month-by-month: 3 jobs for January, February, March
fields.correctAnswer: Process one date partition per job, running 90 sequential jobs. Each job reads one day from Bronze, transforms with the fixed code, and overwrites that day's Silver partition.
fields.distractorRationale: Option 1 is risky: a single job that fails at day 45 loses all progress. Option 3 doesn't provide the isolation needed. Option 4 is viable but coarser: a failure at day 15 of February loses that entire month's progress. Per-day jobs provide the finest granularity for failure recovery.

fields.label: How does the backfill avoid conflicting with the daily pipeline?
options: Lock the Silver table during backfill

Date-level partitioning provides natural isolation: the daily pipeline writes partition date=2026-03-12, the backfill writes partition date=2026-01-15. They never touch the same partition. No locking needed.
Run the backfill only on weekends when the daily pipeline is paused
Use a separate Silver table and swap after backfill completes
fields.correctAnswer: Date-level partitioning provides natural isolation: the daily pipeline writes partition date=2026-03-12, the backfill writes partition date=2026-01-15. They never touch the same partition. No locking needed.

fields.label: How should the backfill handle a failure at day 45 of 90?
options: Restart from day 1 — there is no way to resume

Days 1–44 are already written to Silver (each job overwrites one partition). Resume from day 45. This is the key advantage of per-day jobs: completed days are durably written and don't need to be re-done.
Roll back all 44 completed days and restart from scratch
Skip day 45 and continue with day 46
fields.correctAnswer: Days 1–44 are already written to Silver (each job overwrites one partition). Resume from day 45. This is the key advantage of per-day jobs: completed days are durably written and don't need to be re-done.

fields.label: How should the team validate the backfill?
options: Spot-check 3 random days manually
For each backfilled date: compare row count against Bronze, verify no NORTH_COAST readings are mapped to NORTH, check null rates. Run the validation as part of each per-day job.
Compare total row count before and after
No validation needed — the fixed pipeline code is correct
fields.correctAnswer: For each backfilled date: compare row count against Bronze, verify no NORTH_COAST readings are mapped to NORTH, check null rates. Run the validation as part of each per-day job.

description.context: NordGrid's Silver pipeline crashed mid-write on March 11th. The pipeline read 1.2M rows from Bronze, transformed them, and started writing to Silver. After writing 8 of 12 output files, the executor running the remaining 4 tasks ran out of disk space and failed. The output directory now contains 8 partial Parquet files. The team must diagnose the state of the data and determine the recovery strategy.
description.task: Examine the pipeline state below. Determine what data is corrupted, whether the partial output is usable, and how to recover.
description.validationHint: Parquet files are immutable once written. The 8 completed files are valid. The 4 unwritten partitions are missing. The question is whether reading the directory gives correct but incomplete results, or corrupt results.

pipelineState.label: State after crash
pipelineState.content:
Write mode: mode('overwrite')
Partitioning: not partitioned (flat directory)
Output directory: silver/readings/

Before crash: directory contained yesterday's complete output (12 files)
During write: Spark began overwriting — deleted old files, started writing new ones
After crash: 8 of 12 new files written. 4 files missing. Old files already deleted.

File listing:
  part-00000.parquet  (complete, 100K rows)
  part-00001.parquet  (complete, 100K rows)
  ...8 files total...
  part-00007.parquet  (complete, 100K rows)
  part-00008.parquet  MISSING
  part-00009.parquet  MISSING
  part-00010.parquet  MISSING
  part-00011.parquet  MISSING

Rows in directory: 800,000 (should be 1,200,000)

fields.label: What is the state of the Silver table after the crash?
options: Corrupt — the 8 partial files contain garbled data
Incomplete but not corrupt — the 8 written files are valid Parquet (each file is complete). But 4 files are missing, so the table has 800K of 1.2M rows. Any read returns valid but incomplete data.
Unchanged — Spark rolls back failed writes automatically
Empty — the overwrite deleted old files and the new files are all invalid
fields.correctAnswer: Incomplete but not corrupt — the 8 written files are valid Parquet (each file is complete). But 4 files are missing, so the table has 800K of 1.2M rows. Any read returns valid but incomplete data.

fields.label: Why did mode('overwrite') make this worse than mode('append') would have been?
options: Overwrite is always worse than append

Overwrite deleted yesterday's complete output BEFORE writing the new output. When the write failed, both the old and new data were lost. Append would have kept yesterday's data and added partial new data — still wrong, but recoverable.
Overwrite and append have the same behavior during crashes
Append would have created duplicates which is worse than missing data
fields.correctAnswer: Overwrite deleted yesterday's complete output BEFORE writing the new output. When the write failed, both the old and new data were lost. Append would have kept yesterday's data and added partial new data — still wrong, but recoverable.

fields.label: What is the recovery strategy?
options: Read the 8 partial files and manually add the missing 400K rows
Re-run the pipeline from scratch: read Bronze again, transform, and write with overwrite. The 8 partial files are replaced with the complete 12-file output.
Restore from a backup of the Silver directory
Use Delta Lake's time travel to revert to the previous version
fields.correctAnswer: Re-run the pipeline from scratch: read Bronze again, transform, and write with overwrite. The 8 partial files are replaced with the complete 12-file output.

fields.label: What would have prevented this data loss entirely?
options: Using append mode instead of overwrite
Using Delta Lake — Delta writes are atomic (all-or-nothing). A failed write doesn't affect the current table version. The old data remains readable until the new write completes successfully.
Writing to a different directory and swapping after completion
Both options 2 and 3 prevent the problem — Delta provides built-in atomicity, and write-then-swap achieves the same result for Parquet
fields.correctAnswer: Both options 2 and 3 prevent the problem — Delta provides built-in atomicity, and write-then-swap achieves the same result for Parquet

tasks.title: Build NordGrid's Complete Incremental Silver Pipeline

description.context: NordGrid needs a production-grade incremental Silver pipeline that handles new readings, late arrivals, and corrections. The pipeline must be idempotent (re-runnable), handle all three batch types correctly, and include validation guards. This combines the patterns from Tasks 1–5 into a single pipeline.
description.task: Design the pipeline's processing strategy, then implement it: read existing Silver + daily batch, merge them correctly using overwrite-partition, validate the output, and store the result. Store the final Silver table in a variable called silver.
description.validationHint: The pipeline must: enable dynamic partition overwrite, process all batch dates (not just new ones), avoid duplicates for corrections, and validate row counts.

requirements.label: Pipeline requirements
requirements.content:
Input: PM9_data_readings_existing.csv (400 rows, 10 dates) + PM9_data_readings_daily_batch.csv (50 rows)
Output: Silver Parquet partitioned by date, idempotent

Processing rules:
  1. All batch rows are processed (no date > max filter)
  2. For dates that overlap with existing: batch rows replace existing rows for the same (meter_id, date)
  3. For new dates: batch rows are inserted
  4. After write: row count guard (output >= 90% of expected)
  5. After write: no duplicate (meter_id, date) combinations

fields.label: What makes this pipeline idempotent?
options: The append mode ensures data is always added
The dynamic overwrite-partition mode: re-running replaces the same date partitions with the same data, producing the same final result regardless of how many times it runs
The pipeline checks for duplicates and skips existing data
The validation guards prevent duplicate writes
fields.correctAnswer: The dynamic overwrite-partition mode: re-running replaces the same date partitions with the same data, producing the same final result regardless of how many times it runs

fields.label: How should corrections be handled in a Parquet-based pipeline (without Delta Lake MERGE)?
options: Ignore corrections — the original reading is always correct

For each affected date: read the existing partition, remove rows whose (meter_id, date) matches a batch row, union with the batch rows, then overwrite the partition. The batch row replaces the original.
Append corrections as additional rows and let downstream dedup
Update the Parquet files in place
fields.correctAnswer: For each affected date: read the existing partition, remove rows whose (meter_id, date) matches a batch row, union with the batch rows, then overwrite the partition. The batch row replaces the original.

starterScaffold.content:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col

spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .appName('nordgrid-incremental-silver') \
    .master('local[*]') \
    .getOrCreate()

# Load data
df_existing = spark.read.csv('PM9_data_readings_existing.csv', header=True, inferSchema=True)
df_batch = spark.read.csv('PM9_data_readings_daily_batch.csv', header=True, inferSchema=True)

# Write existing as partitioned Parquet
df_existing.write.mode('overwrite').partitionBy('date').parquet('output/silver/')

print(f"Existing: {df_existing.count()} rows, dates: {df_existing.select('date').distinct().count()}")
print(f"Batch: {df_batch.count()} rows, dates: {sorted([r.date for r in df_batch.select('date').distinct().collect()])}")

# YOUR SOLUTION BELOW
# Build the complete incremental pipeline:
# 1. Enable dynamic partition overwrite
# 2. For each date in the batch:
#    a. Read existing partition for that date (if exists)
#    b. Remove rows that match batch keys (meter_id, date)
#    c. Union remaining existing rows with batch rows for that date
# 3. Write all affected date partitions with overwrite
# 4. Read back complete Silver table
# 5. Validate: no duplicate (meter_id, date), row count reasonable
# Store in 'silver'



# YOUR SOLUTION ABOVE

# Verification (do not modify)
print(f"Silver total: {silver.count()}")
dupes = silver.groupBy('meter_id', 'date').count().filter('count > 1').count()
print(f"Duplicate (meter_id, date): {dupes}")
print(f"Dates: {silver.select('date').distinct().count()}")

assertions.description: Total row count is reasonable (> 400, existing + new)

conceptsCombined: Late-arrival handling — process all batch dates, not just new (Task 3, Lesson 4)
Correction handling — remove-and-replace for Parquet (Task 3, Lesson 5)
Validation guards — duplicate check, row count (PM6, Lesson 7)
Backfill resilience — per-partition granularity enables resume (Task 4, Lesson 6)
synthesisNotes.rationale: This synthesis combines every production pipeline pattern from the module into a single working pipeline. The student must handle the three batch types (new, late, correction), ensure idempotency, and validate the output. This is the pipeline pattern NordGrid would run in production every morning.

selfCheck.evidenceRealistic: Append-mode duplication, mid-write crash state, and backfill scenarios mirror real production incidents. The three batch types (new, late, correction) represent the standard categories every incremental pipeline must handle.
selfCheck.theoryPracticeBoundary: Theory teaches idempotency, overwrite-partition, and incremental patterns individually. Practice requires combining them into a working pipeline that handles the messy reality of late-arriving data and corrections — a scenario more complex than any single theory lesson.
